I am grateful to Tim Bruckner, Jeffrey Clemens, and Jonathan Meer for helpful comments, and to Allison Emory, Laetitia Lebihan, and Melvin Livingston for clarifying discussions about their papers.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Title: w31191.rev0.pdf URL Source: https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w31191/revisions/w31191.rev0.pdf Published Time: Fri, 08 Dec 2023 21:14:34 GMT Number of Pages: 57 Markdown Content: NBER WORKING PAPER SERIES THE EFFECTS OF MINIMUM ", "A REVIEW OF RECENT EVIDENCE ON HEALTH AND RELATED BEHAVIORS David Neumark Working Paper 31191 http://www.nber.org/papers/w31191 NATIONAL BUREAU OF ECONOMIC RESEARCH 1050 Massachusetts Avenue Cambridge, MA 02138 April 2023 I am also affiliated with IZ", "They have not been peer-reviewed or been subject to the review by the NBER Board of Directors that accompanies official NBER publications. \u00a9 2023 by David Neumark.", "A Review of Recent Evidence on Health and Related Behaviors David Neumark NBER Working Paper No. 31191 April 2023 JEL No.", "The findings on diet and obesity either point to beneficial or null effects, but not negative effects, while other evidence indicates that higher minimum wages increase smoking and reduce exercise."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Title: w31191.rev0.pdf URL Source: https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w31191/revisions/w31191.rev0.pdf Pub"}, {"url": "https://clear.dol.gov/Study/short-run-effects-minimum-wage-employment-and-labor-market-participation-Evidence-individual", "title": "Evidence from an individual-level panel (Boffy-Ramirez, 2019)", "summary": "The study also found that immediately following a minimum wage increase, the probability of labor force participation significantly decreases for all age groups, but there is no increase in individuals working part-time. - The study receives a low evidence rating. This means we are not confident that the estimated effects are attributable to minimum wage increases; other factors are likely to have contributed. Intervention Examined Minimum Wage Increases Features of the Study This study examines the effects of experiencing minimum wage increases throughout a long study period.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The study applied a statistical model to compare employment and labor force participation over time, preceding and following a minimum wage change, using monthly longitudinal data from the 1990 to 2017.", "The study uses the monthly Current Population Basic Surveys (CPS) from 1990 through 2017 to collect data on individual employment and labor force status in each month, in which each individual was sampled up to 8 times.", "Monthly minimum wages by state were gathered from a research webpage (for 1990-2011) and state labor agencies and government websites (for 2012-2017).", "The CLEAR\u2019s guidelines require that the authors must observe outcomes for multiple periods before the intervention to rule out the possibility that participants had increasing or decreasing trends in the outcomes examined before the intervention.", "Nearly 70% participants were observed monthly for 4 consecutive months, and about 20% were observed for 2 or 3 months (those who were observed for one month were excluded from the analysis)."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The study applied a statistical model to compare employment and labor force participation over time, preceding and follo"}, {"url": "https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3161175", "title": "Does Minimum Wage Affect Workplace Safety? by V\u00edt Hradil", "summary": "INSUFFICIENT_CONTENT", "perspectives": [], "key_facts": [], "knowledge_contribution": ""}, {"url": "https://www.epi.org/blog/most-minimum-wage-studies-have-found-little-or-no-job-loss/", "title": "Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss", "summary": "Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum wage increases, largely driven by concerns about job losses. After a minimum wage increase, the story goes, many employers will not be able to afford to pay their workers the new higher minimum wage and will therefore shrink their payrolls. If these job losses are large enough, they could even swamp the higher wages and lead to lower overall wage income for the entire group of affected workers.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum wage increases, largely driven by concerns about job losses.", "A new review that I co-authored with Arindrajit Dube finds that most minimum wage studies find no job losses or only small disemployment effects.", "This conclusion is strengthened by focusing on the studies that examine broad groups of low-wage workers or the overall workforce, not just narrow segments like teenagers.", "As the figure below shows, the median employment response is essentially zero among these more comprehensive studies, with 90% of these studies finding no or only small disemployment effects.", "The OWE estimate ranges are \u201clarge negative\u201d (less than -0.8), \u201cmedium negative\u201d (greater than or equal to -0.8 but less than -0.4), \u201csmall negative\u201d (greater than or equal to -0.4 but less than 0.0), and \u201cpositive\u201d (greater than or equal to 0.0)."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Most minimum wage studies have found little or no job loss There is always a great deal of political heat around minimum"}, {"url": "https://academic.oup.com/qje/article/141/1/373/8376639", "title": "Who's Afraid of the Minimum Wage? Measuring the Impacts on ...", "summary": "- Split View - Views - Cite Cite Nirupama L Rao, Max Risch, Who\u2019s Afraid of the Minimum Wage? Measuring the Impacts on Independent Businesses Using Matched U.S. Tax Returns, The Quarterly Journal of Economics, Volume 141, Issue 1, February 2026, Pages 373\u2013427, https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjaf053 Close - Share Icon Share Abstract A common concern surrounding minimum wage policies is their impact on independent businesses, which are often feared to be less able to bear or pass on cost increases.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Tax Returns, The Quarterly Journal of Economics, Volume 141, Issue 1, February 2026, Pages 373\u2013427, https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjaf053 Close - Share Icon Share Abstract A common concern surrounding minimum wage policies is their impact on independen", "Higher wage floors, however, forestall entry, particularly for less productive firms, reducing the number of independent firms operating in these industries by roughly 2%.", "We find that firms do not lay off employees but modestly reduce hiring in part-time positions, resulting in 1.5 (or 2%) fewer employment relationships per firm per year.", "Higher minimum wages drive up the wage bills of exposed firms by 6% on average, with income gains concentrated among low-earning workers and no evidence of reduced pay to high-earning workers.", "We find that independent firms finance their higher wage bills with new revenue, raising roughly 3.3% more revenue four years after the minimum wage increase."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Tax Returns, The Quarterly Journal of Economics, Volume 141, Issue 1, February 2026, Pages 373\u2013427, https://doi.org/10.1"}]}
